title: Colonial Expansion after 1750: Britain, the Boers and the Frontier
subject: History

meta.title: Colonial Expansion after 1750: Britain, the Boers and the Frontier
meta.subject: History

sources.title: Grade 10 - Topic 5: Colonial expansion in South Africa after 1750
Grade 10 - Topic 4: Transformations in southern Africa after 1750
Grade 10 - What was South Africa like in 1750?
The establishment of the Cape and its impact on the Khoikhoi and Dutch
Dutch settlement, the Indian Ocean slave trade and slavery at the Cape
Early struggles: contact and conflict in the Cape Colony
The Eastern Cape Wars of Dispossession 1779-1878
The Mfecane: understanding a period of transformation in southern Africa
Origins of the Battle of Blood River 1838
The aftermath of the Battle of Blood River 1838
Contact, conflict and dispossession on the Cape eastern or northern frontiers in the nineteenth century
Land: dispossession, resistance and restitution - a history 1600s-1990s
Timeline of land dispossession and segregation in South Africa 1800-1899

slides.heading: Colonial Expansion after 1750
slides.lead: How British and Boer expansion, the Great Trek and frontier war reshaped southern Africa - and dispossessed its African peoples of their land.

image.alt: A map-like landscape with wagon tracks moving inland from a coastal settlement towards distant hills.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='320' height='52' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M0 128 L60 108 L120 122 L190 100 L250 118 L320 98 L320 128 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='14' y='108' width='16' height='16' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M40 132 q60 -18 120 0 t150 -6' fill='none' stroke='#5c1414' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6 6'/><circle cx='268' cy='44' r='22' fill='#e8a13a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Frame the unit at FET level: this is not just a story of trekking wagons but of competing claims to land and power. Establish three interlocking threads - (1) British reform of the Cape Colony, (2) the Boer response and the Great Trek, and (3) the frontier wars through which African societies lost land and independence. Stress that the same events look very different depending on whose perspective we take.

slides.heading: Southern Africa around 1750
slides.lead: By 1750 the region was home to many established, diverse societies - it was never an empty land.

blocks.text: Long before large-scale colonial expansion, southern Africa was densely settled. **Khoikhoi** herders and **San** hunter-gatherers lived across the Cape interior, while powerful **Bantu-speaking** chiefdoms - among them the **Xhosa**, **Zulu**, **Sotho** and **Tswana** - farmed, kept cattle and traded across the eastern half of the region.

items: The **Dutch East India Company (VOC)** ruled a small colony around Cape Town, worked largely by enslaved people.
Cattle, land and control of trade routes were the main sources of wealth and power.
African societies had their own laws, armies and systems of government - they were not passive bystanders.

slides.notes: Directly confront the colonial myth of 'empty land' that was later used to justify dispossession. Emphasise complexity and agency: these were organised polities with strong military and political structures. This baseline matters, because everything that follows is a contest over the land and cattle of societies that were already here.

slides.heading: From Dutch to British control of the Cape
slides.lead: Between 1795 and 1806 the Cape passed from the VOC to Britain - and British priorities were different.

blocks.text: Britain first occupied the Cape in 1795 and took permanent control in **1806**, keeping it after the Napoleonic Wars. For Britain the Cape was above all a strategic **naval station** on the sea route to India, but British rule soon began to change law, labour and land inside the colony.

items: British administrators wanted a more ordered, taxable and governable colony.
English was promoted in courts, schools and government (anglicisation).
These reforms unsettled the Dutch-descended colonists, later called **Boers** or **Afrikaners**.

image.alt: A ship at anchor below Table Mountain with a flag on shore.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='108' width='220' height='52' fill='#a8bcae'/><path d='M20 108 L60 66 L120 62 L170 78 L200 108 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='150' y='96' width='40' height='12' fill='#5c1414'/><rect x='166' y='70' width='3' height='26' fill='#333'/><path d='M169 72 h16 v10 h-16 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='96' y='90' width='36' height='14' fill='#7a5a3a'/><path d='M100 90 v-16 M126 90 v-16' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M100 76 h26 v14 h-26 Z' fill='#dcd3c2'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain why the change of ruler mattered: the VOC had been a trading company; Britain was a global empire with reformist and strategic aims. Anglicisation and administrative reform created friction with the Dutch-speaking colonists. This friction is one root of the Great Trek covered shortly.

slides.heading: Three reforms that angered the Boers
slides.lead: Between 1820 and 1834 Britain changed who could own labour, who had rights, and who could settle the frontier.

blocks.heading: 1820 Settlers
blocks.text: Britain settled several thousand British immigrants on the eastern frontier as a human buffer against the Xhosa, intensifying competition for land.

blocks.heading: Ordinance 50 (1828)
blocks.text: Removed pass restrictions on the Khoikhoi and free black people, granting them legal equality that many Boers resented.

blocks.heading: Abolition of slavery (1834)
blocks.text: Slavery ended across the British Empire; Boer slave-owners felt compensation was too small and paid in distant London.

slides.notes: These three reforms are the classic 'push factors' behind the Great Trek. Frame them analytically: Boer grievances were partly economic (loss of enslaved labour and land) and partly about status and control over African and Khoikhoi people. Note that reforms driven by British humanitarian and imperial motives had, for the Khoikhoi and enslaved, genuinely liberating effects - perspective matters.

slides.heading: Causes of the Great Trek
slides.lead: From 1835 groups of Boers - the Voortrekkers - left the Cape Colony to seek land and independence beyond British rule.

blocks.text: The **Great Trek (1835-1846)** was a series of organised migrations of Dutch-speaking farmers out of the eastern Cape. In his 1837 manifesto, Voortrekker leader **Piet Retief** listed grievances including the loss of enslaved labour, insecurity on the frontier, and interference by British officials and missionaries.

items: **Push factors:** anglicisation, the end of slavery, Ordinance 50, land shortage and frontier war.
**Pull factors:** the promise of free grazing land and self-government in the interior.
Leaders included Retief, **Gert Maritz**, **Andries Pretorius** and **Andries Hendrik Potgieter**.

image.alt: An ox-wagon drawn by a team of oxen moving to the right.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='120' width='240' height='40' fill='#c8b89a'/><path d='M150 74 q30 -30 60 0 v34 h-60 Z' fill='#dcd3c2' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='3'/><rect x='150' y='104' width='60' height='6' fill='#7a5a3a'/><circle cx='162' cy='120' r='11' fill='none' stroke='#5c1414' stroke-width='4'/><circle cx='200' cy='120' r='11' fill='none' stroke='#5c1414' stroke-width='4'/><g fill='#7a5a3a'><rect x='96' y='96' width='30' height='16'/><rect x='60' y='96' width='30' height='16'/></g><path d='M126 104 h24' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M60 100 l-10 -6 M60 108 l-10 6' stroke='#333' stroke-width='2'/></svg>

slides.notes: Distinguish push and pull factors clearly - a common CAPS exam frame. Read Retief's manifesto grievances at a critical distance: they reveal what the Voortrekkers wanted (land and control of labour free from British restraint). Avoid presenting the Trek as heroic destiny; it was one group's response to losing privileges, with heavy consequences for African societies in its path.

slides.heading: The routes of the Great Trek
slides.lead: Voortrekker parties moved north and east into land already occupied by African societies.

blocks.text: The trekkers crossed the **Orange River** and fanned out onto the highveld and towards **Natal**. They did not move into empty country: they entered the territories of the **Ndebele** under Mzilikazi, the **Zulu** under Dingane, and Sotho-Tswana chiefdoms, which made conflict almost certain.

items: Potgieter's party clashed with the **Ndebele**, defeating them at Vegkop (1836) and Mosega (1837).
Retief's party crossed the Drakensberg into **Natal** to seek land from the Zulu king Dingane.
The migration spread colonial-style land claims deep into the interior.

image.alt: A simple map showing arrows moving north and east from a coastal point, crossing a river line into hilly interior.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#eef2e2'/><path d='M0 118 q60 -14 120 -4 t120 -10' fill='none' stroke='#4a6fa5' stroke-width='4'/><rect x='24' y='120' width='14' height='14' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M40 124 q40 -50 96 -60' fill='none' stroke='#5c1414' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6 5'/><path d='M136 64 l10 -2 -6 9 Z' fill='#5c1414'/><path d='M40 124 q80 -20 150 12' fill='none' stroke='#5c1414' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6 5'/><path d='M190 136 l8 -6 1 11 Z' fill='#5c1414'/><path d='M150 60 l14 -16 14 16 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M178 66 l12 -14 12 14 Z' fill='#7a8b5a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Reinforce the theme of overlapping land claims. The trek routes carried the trekkers straight into powerful kingdoms, so the wars that follow were not accidents but the predictable result of moving armed settler groups into occupied land. Keep the African polities in the foreground, not just as obstacles to the trek.

slides.heading: Retief, Dingane and the road to war
slides.lead: A land agreement with the Zulu king ended in the killing of Retief's party.

blocks.text: Piet Retief negotiated with the Zulu king **Dingane** for land in Natal. After a treaty was reportedly signed, Dingane had Retief and his men killed at his capital, **uMgungundlovu**, in February 1838, and Zulu forces attacked trekker camps. From the Zulu view, armed newcomers claiming Zulu land were a direct threat to the kingdom's survival.

items: Both sides distrusted the other's intentions over land and cattle.
The killings and raids led the Voortrekkers to prepare for a major battle.
This set the scene for the Battle of Blood River later in 1838.

slides.notes: Present competing interpretations rather than a single villain: Voortrekker accounts stress treachery, while from Dingane's side the trekkers were a strategic danger to Zulu independence, just as they had been to the Ndebele. Encourage learners to weigh motive and evidence. Flag that some details of the treaty are historically debated.

slides.heading: The Battle of Blood River (Ncome)
slides.lead: A laager of Voortrekkers defeated a large Zulu army - a battle still contested in memory today.

blocks.text: On 16 December 1838 about **470 Voortrekkers** under **Andries Pretorius**, defending a **laager** (a circle of wagons) with muskets and cannon, defeated a much larger Zulu army beside the Ncome River. Zulu casualties were very heavy while trekker losses were tiny, and the river was said to run red - giving the battle its name. [SME: verify casualty figures.]

items: The trekkers had made a religious vow before the battle, later marked as the Day of the Vow.
The victory opened Natal to Voortrekker settlement and weakened Dingane's power.
Today the site is remembered from two perspectives - as **Blood River** and as **Ncome** - reflected in the Day of Reconciliation.

image.alt: A circle of wagons forming a defensive laager beside a river.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M0 20 q40 20 0 40 q-30 40 20 60 q30 30 0 40' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='8' transform='translate(190 0)'/><circle cx='104' cy='84' r='46' fill='none' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='4' stroke-dasharray='10 6'/><g fill='#7a5a3a'><rect x='96' y='34' width='16' height='8'/><rect x='146' y='60' width='16' height='8'/><rect x='140' y='104' width='16' height='8'/><rect x='96' y='126' width='16' height='8'/><rect x='46' y='104' width='16' height='8'/><rect x='44' y='60' width='16' height='8'/></g><circle cx='104' cy='84' r='6' fill='#5c1414'/></svg>

slides.notes: Handle this sensitive battle carefully. Explain the military reason for the outcome: firearms and cannon behind a fortified laager against fighters armed mainly with spears. Address memory directly - the same event is commemorated by different communities in very different ways, now folded into the Day of Reconciliation (16 December). This is an excellent moment to discuss how history is remembered, not just what happened. Casualty numbers vary between sources; confirm before teaching exact figures.

slides.heading: A century of frontier wars, 1779-1878
slides.lead: On the Cape's eastern frontier, colonists and the Xhosa fought a long series of wars over land and cattle.

blocks.text: Separate from the Great Trek, the **Cape Colony** fought a series of wars against the **Xhosa** along the eastern frontier - sometimes called the Frontier Wars or the **Wars of Dispossession (1779-1878)**. War by war, the colony pushed the frontier eastward and took Xhosa land, cattle and independence.

items: Control of grazing land and cattle theft on both sides drove repeated conflict.
The British used forts, settlers and troops to hold and expand the frontier.
By 1878 Xhosa resistance was broken and their land absorbed into the colony.

image.alt: A frontier line shifting eastwards over time, shown as three coloured bands moving right.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='0' y='24' width='80' height='112' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='80' y='24' width='60' height='112' fill='#9aac74'/><rect x='140' y='24' width='60' height='112' fill='#c0cf98'/><rect x='76' y='20' width='4' height='120' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='136' y='20' width='4' height='120' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M84 40 l14 6 -14 6 Z' fill='#5c1414'/><path d='M144 40 l14 6 -14 6 Z' fill='#5c1414'/><text x='16' y='150' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#333'>1779</text><text x='176' y='150' font-family='sans-serif' font-size='12' fill='#333'>1878</text></svg>

slides.notes: The frontier wars are easy to overlook next to the dramatic Great Trek, but they are central to the CAPS theme of dispossession. Stress the pattern rather than every individual war: cumulatively the frontier moved east and the Xhosa lost their land and sovereignty. Grahamstown/Makhanda grew as a colonial military and settler centre on this frontier.

slides.heading: The Mfecane / Difaqane
slides.lead: Political upheaval among African societies overlapped with colonial expansion - and its causes are debated.

blocks.text: In the same period, the **Mfecane** (Difaqane) saw wars, migrations and the rise and fall of kingdoms across the interior, linked to the growth of the **Zulu** state under Shaka and to competition for land and trade. Older accounts blamed the Zulu alone; many historians now argue that pressures such as trade, drought and the demand for labour and cattle - some tied to colonial expansion - also played a part. [SME: historiography of the Mfecane is contested.]

items: The upheaval reshaped where African societies lived and how strong they were.
Colonists sometimes wrongly claimed this left 'empty' land free for the taking.
In truth, land emptied by conflict was still claimed and remembered by African peoples.

slides.notes: This is a genuinely contested topic - handle it as historiography, an FET-appropriate skill. Warn learners against the old 'empty land' myth that used the Mfecane to justify Boer land claims. Present the shift from a single-cause (Shaka) explanation to a multi-cause one that includes colonial and economic pressures. Encourage learners to see that historians revise interpretations as evidence and viewpoints change.

slides.heading: The Boer republics
slides.lead: Out of the trek and its wars came Boer-ruled states in the interior.

blocks.text: The Voortrekkers set up their own states. Their republic in **Natal (Natalia)** was annexed by Britain in **1843**, but inland the Boers established the **Orange Free State** and the **South African Republic (Transvaal)**, whose independence Britain recognised in the Sand River (1852) and Bloemfontein (1854) conventions.

items: These republics were built on land taken from African societies.
They denied political rights to African, Khoikhoi and Coloured people.
Britain and the Boer republics would later clash over control of the interior.

image.alt: Two simple flag shapes representing inland Boer republics beside a coastal British-held zone.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='24' y='40' width='60' height='40' fill='#dcd3c2' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='24' y='40' width='60' height='13' fill='#e8a13a'/><rect x='24' y='67' width='60' height='13' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='24' y='40' width='4' height='80' fill='#5c1414'/><rect x='140' y='40' width='60' height='40' fill='#dcd3c2' stroke='#7a5a3a' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='140' y='40' width='60' height='13' fill='#4a6fa5'/><rect x='140' y='67' width='60' height='13' fill='#7a8b5a'/><rect x='140' y='40' width='4' height='80' fill='#5c1414'/><rect y='128' width='240' height='32' fill='#a8bcae'/></svg>

slides.notes: Connect this slide forward to Topic 6 (the South African War and Union): the republics created here are the states Britain will fight in 1899-1902. Emphasise that these were settler-colonial states resting on dispossession and racial exclusion, so their founding is part of the dispossession story, not separate from it.

slides.heading: Dispossession and its legacy
slides.lead: The central outcome of colonial expansion after 1750 was the loss of African land and independence.

blocks.text: Across the frontier and the interior, colonial expansion transferred **land, cattle and power** from African societies to colonists. African polities lost sovereignty, farmers were pushed onto smaller and poorer areas, and many were drawn into working for colonists - patterns later hardened by laws such as the 1913 Natives Land Act.

items: Land was the key resource - and its loss shaped African life for generations.
Dispossession created deep and lasting inequality in South Africa.
These nineteenth-century events are the root of land debates that continue today.

slides.notes: Zoom out to the big picture and the 'so what'. Make the through-line explicit: the frontier wars and trek did not just move borders, they built a structure of dispossession that later apartheid law extended. This links the unit to learners' present - the ongoing land question - which is exactly the analytical connection FET History asks for.

slides.heading: Colonial expansion, 1806-1854

blocks.alt: Timeline of colonial expansion from British occupation of the Cape in 1806 to the Bloemfontein Convention in 1854.

events.label: Britain takes permanent control of the Cape
British 1820 Settlers placed on the eastern frontier
Ordinance 50 frees the Khoikhoi from pass laws
Slavery abolished in the British Empire
Retief killed; Battle of Blood River (Ncome)
Boer republics recognised (Transvaal, Orange Free State)

slides.notes: Use this to consolidate cause and effect before the review. Ask learners to sort the dates into causes (1820, 1828, 1834), the trek itself (1835), conflict (1838) and outcomes (1852-1854). This structure mirrors how CAPS source and essay questions are framed.

slides.heading: Reviewing colonial expansion after 1750
slides.lead: Expansion, resistance and dispossession seen from more than one perspective.

blocks.text: British reform of the Cape helped push the Boers into the interior; the Great Trek and the frontier wars carried colonial power inland; and African societies - Xhosa, Zulu, Ndebele, Sotho and Tswana - resisted but were dispossessed of land and independence. Reading these events from different perspectives is a core skill of FET History.

blocks.label: Q1
blocks.question: Explain how British reforms at the Cape (the 1820 Settlers, Ordinance 50 and the abolition of slavery) contributed to the Great Trek.

blocks.label: Q2
blocks.question: Using the Battle of Blood River (Ncome), explain how the same historical event can be understood differently depending on perspective.

blocks.label: Q3
blocks.question: To what extent was dispossession of land the main outcome of colonial expansion in South Africa after 1750? Support your answer with evidence.

slides.notes: Close by returning to the three threads from slide 1 and the theme of perspective. The three questions rise in cognitive demand - explain, compare perspectives, then argue an extent-of judgement - matching CAPS source-based and extended-writing expectations. Q3 works well as an essay-planning task. Remind learners this unit sets up Topic 6: the South African War and the making of the Union of South Africa.
